channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Putin Turned Japan Into a Den of Spies
item.description: Operating out of a Tokyo high-rise, a military intelligence unit finds the high-tech equipment that Russia needs to wage war.
item.dc:creator: Jane Bradley, Michael Schwirtz and Adam Goldman
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Aeroflot office in Toranomon Kotohira Tower in Tokyo. Japan’s weak espionage laws and flourishing high-tech industry have made it a crucial base for the Russian war effort.

item.title: Inside the Secret Factory That Supplies Ukraine’s War Drones
item.description: With its mass-produced and inexpensive A.I. powered war machines, the German start-up Helsing SE illustrates a profound shift in military spending.
item.dc:creator: Vivienne Walt
item.media:credit: Patrick Junker for The New York Times
item.media:description: Helsing SE, which makes autonomous jets and drones, is the most valuable A.I. defense start-up in Europe.

item.title: Trump Accounts Prompt Parents to Start Saving Early for Their Kids
item.description: Between Trump accounts and child-friendly investment platforms, it has become much easier for parents to invest for their children’s future.
item.dc:creator: Charlotte Cowles
item.media:credit: Chase Castor for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mallory Baska, who owns a financial education services business, is opening a Trump account for her youngest son, who was born in 2025 and qualifies for the $1,000 government seed deposit.

item.title: Kansas City, the Smallest World Cup Host, Has the World at Its Feet
item.description: The city has a proud history with the sport thanks to Lamar Hunt, who owned the Kansas City Chiefs and co-founded Major League Soccer.
item.dc:creator: Joe Drape
item.media:credit: Christopher Smith for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans of the Dutch World Cup soccer team in a downtown district of Kansas City, Mo.

item.title: What Slate Auto’s $25,000 Electric Pickup Truck Is Like in Person
item.description: The Slate Auto truck is making a splash for its lack of frills and its relatively affordable price, but it’s jumping into a difficult market.
item.dc:creator: Jamie Lincoln Kitman and Tag Christof
item.media:description: While the base truck comes unpainted in gray, many color wrap options are available.

item.title: What’s in the Housing Bill That Just Became Law
item.description: It is no Great Society measure, but the new law offers local governments and builders incentives to make incremental changes.
item.dc:creator: Conor Dougherty
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act encourages “build to rent” communities like this one in Simpsonville, S.C.

item.title: Kids Can’t Stop Watching ‘Moana.’ There’s a Scientific Explanation.
item.description: The 2016 release has become the most watched movie on Disney+. Parents and experts explain why kids can’t get enough.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Streams of “Moana” on Disney+ total more than 1.5 billion hours.

item.title: Times Journalists Subpoenaed as Trump Escalates Pressure on Media
item.description: The Justice Department is seeking to compel testimony from reporters who wrote about the new Air Force One. The Times called the move a “brazen act.”
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:description: President Trump speaking to reporters before boarding the new Air Force One last week.

item.title: New York Times Accuses Federal Employment Agency of Retaliation
item.description: A May lawsuit by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission claims the newspaper discriminated against a white male employee who did not get a promotion.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times filed a counterclaim against the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission in federal court on Friday.

item.title: SK Hynix Stock Rises on First Day of US Trading
item.description: SK Hynix began trading at $170 a share, above its initial public offering price of $149, in the latest test of investor demand for A.I.-related companies.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.media:description: Kwak Noh-Jung, the SK Hynix chief executive, in New York City for the company’s initial public offering on the Nasdaq.

item.title: Shippers Face Deepening Dilemma as U.S. and Iran Vie for Control of the Strait
item.description: Companies desperately want to get their ships moving through the Strait of Hormuz, but face mounting risks. “Things are becoming uglier by the minute,” one executive said.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis, Jenny Gross and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ships anchored off Oman’s coast near the Strait of Hormuz. Ship operators in the area are eager to resume normal business but face great peril if they do.

item.title: Atlassian Illegally Fired Worker in Rare Legal Victory for Tech Worker
item.description: A federal labor law judge determined last week that the software maker Atlassian had illegally fired an employee who questioned company policy changes.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber
item.media:description: Denise Unterwurzacher was fired by Atlassian in 2023.

item.title: A.I. Has Rewarded Investors. It May Now Pose Their Greatest Risk.
item.description: Near the midpoint of the year, stocks and bonds both report good returns. But the global stock market has become highly concentrated, our columnist says.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer

item.title: How Volkswagen’s Troubles Were Made in China
item.description: The German auto giant’s push into China powered it for decades, but now the company faces fierce competition from Chinese automakers in markets around the world.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Volkswagen booth at the Shanghai auto show. For many years, China generated at least half of the German car company’s profits.

item.title: The Work of Helping A.I. Destroy Work
item.description: Start-ups are paying white-collar professionals to teach their jobs to artificial intelligence models. It’s a bonanza. It’s bleak. Where will it end?
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: Wall St. Sets Limits on Prediction Market Trading
item.description: Goldman Sachs is the latest firm to restrict how employees use the popular betting platforms.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Goldman Sachs is limiting how employees can trade on prediction markets.

item.title: Meta Ordered by E.U. to Alter ‘Addictive Design’ of Instagram and Facebook
item.description: European Union authorities said the company’s use of “addictive design” violated a digital safety law.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:credit: Dia Takacsova for The New York Times
item.media:description: The preliminary ruling against Meta is the latest effort by the European Commission in Brussels to confront social media companies over some of the ways they hook users.

item.title: You’re Freezing in Your Office Building. Here’s Why.
item.description: Office temperatures are typically set in the low 70s during summer. But where you sit and the efficiency of the cooling system can make you feel colder.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni

item.title: Hotel Workers Hoped the World Cup Would Boost Their Hours. It Didn’t.
item.description: Even as matches have drawn large crowds, hotel bookings have lagged. And workers in host cities say they’re struggling.
item.dc:creator: Kurtis Lee
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Santos González Lobos, 61, works as a kitchen cleaner at the Westin Bonaventure Hotel & Suites in Los Angeles. “The end of the month comes and you think, ‘It won’t be enough,’” he said.

item.title: The Message From Barcelona’s New Sustainable Tourism Leader: ‘Not One Tourist More.’
item.description: As the commissioner for sustainable tourism, José Antonio Donaire’s job is to diminish the impact of the nearly 16 million annual visitors to the Catalan capital. Here’s what he’s planning.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Abend
item.media:credit: Edu Bayer for The New York Times
item.media:description: José Antonio Donaire, Barcelona’s new commissioner for sustainable tourism, is developing strategies to control runaway tourism in the city, including a plan to make the popular Boqueria market, above, a place where residents actually want to shop again.

item.title: Oil Prices Wobble As a Week of Hostilities Derails Shipping
item.description: The latest turmoil, sparked by attacks on three commercial ships in the strait days ago, pushed the price of crude back above prewar levels.
item.dc:creator: Jason Karaian

item.title: John Deere Farm Equipment Owners Have Right-to-Repair, F.T.C. Says
item.description: A settlement by the company with the Federal Trade Commission will allow farmers and local mechanics to make their own fixes, instead of relying on authorized dealers.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.media:description: Danny Ward disconnects an implement from a John Deere combine during winter wheat harvest near Skedee, Oklahoma, in 2024.

item.title: Delta, United and Other Airlines Are Offering Lower-Cost Business Class Options: What to Know
item.description: Delta is the latest U.S. airline to introduce stripped down, luxury-class fares. What do you get for your money, and is it worth it? What you need to know.
item.dc:creator: Victoria M. Walker
item.media:description: United Airlines has been offering reduced-price business class service since April, with fewer perks.

item.title: Volkswagen Is Cutting Production as Sales in China Plunge
item.description: The German automaker has struggled to compete with fast-growing Chinese companies that offer more affordable and sophisticated electric vehicles.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing, Theresa Rauffmann and Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Ingmar Nolting for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Volkswagen factory in Zwickau, Germany, in 2024. The carmaker is a crucial part of the country’s industrial base.

item.title: Warsh Announces Slate of New Advisers for Fed Task Forces
item.description: The five task forces are focused on topics that Kevin M. Warsh, the chairman of the Federal Reserve, has said are “central to the broad conduct of monetary policy.”
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Ben Casselman
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The task forces span topics that Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chairman, has said are “central to the broad conduct of monetary policy.”

item.title: China, Russia and Others Seek to Inflame Debate Over A.I. Data Centers
item.description: State actors in China, Russia and Iran have sought to exploit the U.S. public debate over the effects of the technology.
item.dc:creator: Steven Lee Myers and Dustin Volz
item.media:description: OpenAI released this cartoon as an example of one generated with ChatGPT by people in China who the company believed were affiliated with a regional government. It is not clear what word in the title was blurred out.

item.title: Grocery Stores Lower Prices as Consumers Pare Spending
item.description: While shoppers may get better deals on some items, it’s unlikely their overall grocery bill will fall.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell and Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Consumers are making fewer trips to traditional grocery stores in favor of discount chains like Aldi, which opened a store in Westlake, Fla., last year as part of its U.S. expansion.

item.title: A $3.2 Trillion Deal-Making Frenzy Is Spurred by the A.I. Economy
item.description: This year’s boom includes the most spent on global deal-making in a six-month period in a decade. But questions persist about whether it can continue.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The frenzy has heavily favored large companies, with 44 deals announced in the first six months of this year that were larger than $10 billion.

item.title: Renewed Fighting With Iran Shows Cracks in Peace-Trade Rally
item.description: Investors have taken heart from the fragile cease-fire between the United States and Iran before. Will they do so again?
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ship traffic has slowed drastically in the vital Strait of Hormuz trade conduit as fighting resumed.

item.title: Help! I Refueled My Rental Car, but Enterprise Charged Me Anyway.
item.description: A traveler to Germany returned his vehicle with slightly more gas than it had when he picked it up. So why was he billed $131 to fill up the tank?
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel

item.title: Can Xenom Turn Working Out Into a Sport?
item.description: Xenom, a new fitness company, wants to make weekend warriors feel like pros.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh and Jake Dockins
item.media:credit: Jake Dockins for The New York Times

item.title: The World Cup’s Biggest Match Is Nike Vs. Adidas
item.description: Second place in the hearts and wallets of Americans isn’t good enough for the world’s largest sportswear companies, who bet big on the tournament.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin and Emma Rose Milligan
item.media:credit: Emma Rose Milligan for The New York Times

item.title: Oil Prices Remain Elevated Amid Shipping Traffic Slowdown in Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Fresh data showed that the number of ships moving through the Strait of Hormuz halved on Wednesday amid renewed fighting.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: At Warsh’s First Fed Meeting, Some Officials Signaled Support for Raising Interest Rates
item.description: Minutes from Kevin M. Warsh’s first meeting as chairman showed heightened unease about inflation, which is running at a three-year high.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Minutes from Kevin M. Warsh’s first Federal Reserve meeting as chairman were significantly pared down compared with those released under his predecessors.

item.title: William D. Zabel, High-Flying Lawyer and Civil Rights Champion, Dies at 89
item.description: He brokered celebrity divorces and recovered billions for victims of Bernie Madoff’s Ponzi scheme. But he was proudest of helping to legalize interracial marriage.
item.dc:creator: Sam Roberts
item.media:description: William D. Zabel in 2007. Justice Sonia Sotomayor of the U.S. Supreme Court said he had “remained true to the lawyer’s responsibilities to help secure a just world.”

item.title: Oil Market Calm Shattered by Fresh Hostilities Between US and Iran
item.description: Oil prices are likely to remain volatile as long as the United States and Iran are unable to resolve the status of the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Air Canada Chooses Anko van der Werff, Who Speaks French, as CEO After Language Backlash
item.description: Michael Rousseau, who prompted widespread criticism with a largely English condolence video following a crash in March, will retire.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Anko van der Werff, who will take over as chief executive of Air Canada in the fall, in Copenhagen last month.

item.title: In San Francisco, Some Home Sellers Now Ask for OpenAI or Anthropic Stock
item.description: Even before OpenAI and Anthropic go public, they are distorting home sales in the San Francisco Bay Area, as people race to buy and sellers ask for stock instead of cash.
item.dc:creator: Emmy Martin
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times
item.media:description: San Francisco home sales in May rose 12.2 percent from a year earlier, with the median price of a single-family home climbing to $2.14 million, according to a Homes.com report.

item.title: Lawmakers Target the Power of Health Insurance Giants
item.description: UnitedHealth Group, CVS Health and Cigna are battling efforts to break up their control over prescription drugs.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson and Rebecca Robbins

item.title: Trump Lashes Out at Spain: ‘Cut Off All Trade.’ Here’s Why That’s Unlikely.
item.description: A move against Spain would face political, legal and practical issues and probably provoke a wider trade war with the European Union. It could also roil markets.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump makes remarks as he meets with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte at the NATO meetings in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Global Economic Output Looks Slower for 2026, IMF Says
item.description: The I.M.F. projected world output growth would fall to 3 percent for the year, a number pushed down by high commodity prices.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Buildings destroyed by U.S.-Israeli airstrikes in Tehran in March. Despite dark clouds, the global economy has proved relatively resilient, buttressed partly by demand for A.I. technology.

item.title: Jeff Bezos’s Blue Origin Nears a Big Fund-Raising Round
item.description: The rocket company founded by Jeff Bezos is set to take on external investors for the first time, at a $130 billion valuation.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: Jeff Bezos’ Blue Origin is gaining its first outside investors.

item.title: A New ‘Little House’ Broadens and Updates the Prairie
item.description: A Netflix adaptation of the beloved tale empowers Ma and adds new Black and Native characters to challenge prejudices. The creators know criticism is coming.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)
Little House on the Prairie (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Karen Asher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Netflix’s “Little House on the Prairie” was filmed in Winnipeg. The new Ingalls family includes Skywalker Hughes as Mary, left, and Alice Halsey as Laura.

item.title: EU Rejects Plea to Pause Troubled EES Biometrics System at Airports Ahead of Summer Rush
item.description: E.U. leaders rebuffed a plea from the aviation industry to pause the biometric checkpoints, which have caused long lines and missed flights. Summer travelers beware.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:description: The Entry/Exit System, fully in place since April, requires visitors to the 29 Schengen area countries to provide biometrics like face photos and fingerprints upon entry and exit.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump After Trump Says Deal With Iran Is ‘Over’
item.description: Attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz sparked a new cycle of retaliation that could throttle the flow of energy from the region.
item.dc:creator: Jason Karaian and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Germans Don’t Love Budweiser. It Won’t Take No for an Answer.
item.description: Not for the first time, Budweiser is trying to make it in Germany. But this is a tough time to sell German drinkers on the image and taste of American beer.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: A box of Anheuser-Busch Bud beer at a supermarket in Berlin.

item.title: Is ‘The View’ a News Show? ABC Says That’s Already Settled.
item.description: In a new filing, ABC said that the F.C.C. had ruled in 2002 that “The View” was a “bona fide news program.”
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Jim Rutenberg
item.media:description: This image released by ABC shows Vice President JD Vance, center, with co-hosts, from left, Whoopi Goldberg, Sara Haines, Joy Behar, Ana Navarro, Sunny Hostin and Alyssa Farah Griffin during an appearance on “The View” in New York last month.

item.title: Mariska Hargitay to Host the Emmys
item.description: The “Law & Order: SVU” star is a departure from the comedians and sitcom actors who have typically hosted the annual awards show.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.media:credit: Kobe Wagstaff for The New York Times
item.media:description: The “Law & Order: SVU” star Mariska Hargitay will host the 78th Emmy Awards in September.

item.title: Trump Promised a Foreign Investment Boom. It’s Getting Harder to Deliver.
item.description: The threat of tariffs may have helped push more capital into the United States, but other factors are pushing it away.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump and Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi last year in Yokosuka, Japan. The country has long been the biggest source of foreign investment for the United States.

item.title: U.S. Trade Deficit Widens in May on Record Goods Imports
item.description: Imports of foreign goods, including pharmaceuticals and equipment for data centers, hit a record high, pushing the monthly trade deficit to its highest level in over a year.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
